title: AI Product Strategy
description: Learn to evaluate AI opportunities, define success metrics, and build compelling product strategies — from identifying the right use cases to communicating AI capabilities to stakeholders.
learningObjectives: Build and prioritize an AI feature roadmap grounded in real user value and engineering feasibility
Define meaningful success metrics that capture AI feature performance beyond traditional product KPIs
Communicate AI capabilities, limitations, and tradeoffs effectively to engineering, leadership, and customers

sections.title: Building an AI Feature Roadmap
sections.content:
Building an AI feature roadmap is fundamentally different from a traditional product roadmap. AI features carry more uncertainty, require iteration cycles that don't fit neatly into sprints, and often improve gradually rather than shipping as "done."

**Start with the problem, not the technology.** The most common mistake PMs make is starting with "we should add AI" instead of "our users struggle with X." Before any AI feature hits your roadmap, you should be able to answer: What user pain point does this solve, and why is Claude the right tool for it?

**The Build vs. Buy Decision**

For every AI capability, you have three options:

• **Use Claude's API directly** — Best for custom workflows, unique data, or tight product integration. You control the experience but own the complexity.
• **Use a pre-built solution** — Third-party tools built on top of LLMs. Faster to ship but less differentiation and control.
• **Don't build it at all** — Sometimes the non-AI solution is better. A well-designed filter can outperform a natural language search if your users know what they're looking for.

**Prioritization Framework for AI Features**

Rank each candidate AI feature on four dimensions:

• **User value** — How painful is the problem today? How frequently do users hit it?
• **Feasibility** — Can Claude handle this reliably today? Do you have the data and infrastructure?
• **Risk tolerance** — What happens when the AI is wrong? A wrong product recommendation is annoying. A wrong medical summary is dangerous.
• **Iteration speed** — How quickly can you test and improve the feature? Prefer features where you can measure quality and iterate on prompts without redeploying.

**Sequencing matters.** Ship AI features that build user trust first — lower-stakes, high-visibility wins like summarization or smart categorization. Once users trust the AI, you can introduce higher-stakes features like automated actions or decision support.

sections.title: Defining Success Metrics for AI Features
sections.content:
Traditional product metrics don't fully capture whether an AI feature is working. You need a layered approach that measures accuracy, user impact, and business value — because an AI feature can be technically accurate but still useless to users.

**Layer 1: Output Quality Metrics**

These measure whether Claude is producing good outputs:

• **Accuracy / Correctness** — What percentage of outputs are factually correct or match expected results? This requires a human evaluation process, at least initially.
• **Hallucination rate** — How often does Claude generate information that isn't grounded in the provided context? Track this explicitly.
• **Consistency** — Does the same input produce reliably similar outputs? Some variance is fine for creative tasks, but structured outputs should be stable.

**Layer 2: User Experience Metrics**

• **Acceptance rate** — When Claude suggests something, how often do users accept it vs. edit or dismiss it? This is your single most important metric for most AI features.
• **Edit distance** — When users modify Claude's output, how much do they change? Small edits mean Claude is close. Complete rewrites mean it's missing the mark.
• **Time saved** — Does the AI feature actually reduce the time to complete the task? Measure the full workflow, not just the generation step.

**Layer 3: Business Impact Metrics**

• **Task completion rate** — Are users completing the overall job more often with the AI feature?
• **Retention and engagement** — Do users who interact with AI features come back more often?
• **Cost per outcome** — What does each Claude API call cost relative to the value it delivers?

**Good vs. Bad AI Metrics**

• Bad: "Claude responded in under 2 seconds" — Latency matters, but speed without quality is worthless.
• Good: "78% of Claude-generated summaries were accepted without edits" — Directly measures value.
• Bad: "We processed 10,000 requests through Claude" — Volume isn't impact.
• Good: "Support agents resolved tickets 40% faster when using Claude-suggested responses" — Ties AI to a business outcome.

sections.title: Communicating AI to Stakeholders
sections.content:
As a PM, you sit at the intersection of engineering, leadership, and customers — each of whom has a different relationship with AI. Your job is to translate between these groups without overpromising or underselling.

**Talking to Leadership**

Executives often oscillate between "AI will change everything" and "why isn't it perfect yet?" Your job is to anchor them in reality.

• **Frame AI as a capability, not a feature.** Instead of "we're adding AI," say "we're reducing the time it takes customers to draft reports by 60%." Leaders care about outcomes, not technology.
• **Set expectations about iteration.** AI features improve over time through prompt refinement, user feedback, and model updates. The v1 won't be the final version, and that's by design.
• **Be upfront about costs.** Claude API calls cost money. Model inference at scale is a real line item. Present the cost-per-value equation, not just the cost.

**Talking to Customers**

• **Never say "AI-powered" and leave it at that.** Tell users what the feature actually does: "This summary was generated automatically from your meeting transcript. You can edit it before sharing."
• **Build in transparency.** Let users see when Claude generated something. Give them easy ways to edit, regenerate, or dismiss AI output. Trust comes from control.
• **Manage the "magic" expectations.** Users who've seen AI demos expect perfection. Set the right frame: "This feature handles about 80% of cases automatically. For the rest, it gives you a starting point."

**The Limitation Communication Framework**

When explaining what Claude can't do, use this structure:

• **What it does well** — Start positive and specific. "Claude is excellent at analyzing your customer feedback and identifying themes."
• **Where it has boundaries** — Be honest without being defeatist. "It works best with English text and may miss nuance in highly technical jargon."
• **How you're addressing it** — Show the path forward. "We're adding domain-specific examples to improve accuracy in your industry's terminology."

sections.title: Scoping AI Features with Engineering
sections.content:
Scoping AI features requires a different conversation with engineering than traditional features. The uncertainty is higher, the iteration cycles are different, and the PM needs to understand enough about Claude's capabilities to make informed tradeoff decisions.

**Prompt Engineering Decisions PMs Should Care About**

You don't need to write the prompts yourself, but you need to understand the tradeoffs:

• **System prompt design** — The system prompt defines Claude's personality, constraints, and behavior in your product. As a PM, you should review and approve system prompts because they directly shape the user experience.
• **Output format** — Should Claude return structured JSON or natural language? This decision affects both the user experience and engineering complexity. Structured output is more reliable for programmatic use; natural language is better for user-facing content.
• **Guardrails and boundaries** — What should Claude refuse to do in your product? What topics should it avoid? These are product decisions, not engineering decisions.

**Understanding Cost and Latency Tradeoffs**

• **Model selection matters.** Claude Haiku is fast and cheap — great for real-time autocomplete or classification. Claude Sonnet balances capability and speed for most product features. Claude Opus is the most capable but slower and more expensive — reserve it for complex reasoning tasks.
• **Input length affects cost.** Every token in the prompt costs money. If you're sending a 50-page document to Claude on every request, that adds up. Work with engineering to optimize what context gets sent.
• **Streaming vs. complete responses.** Streaming shows users output as it's generated, making Claude feel faster even though total time is the same. For user-facing features, streaming is almost always the right choice.

**Scoping the Iteration Plan**

Unlike traditional features, AI features ship with a built-in iteration plan:

• **V1: Prompt-only approach.** Start with well-crafted prompts and Claude's base capabilities. This ships fast and gives you real user data.
• **V2: Refined with real data.** Use actual user interactions to identify failure modes and improve prompts.
• **V3: Infrastructure investment.** Add retrieval (RAG), fine-tuning, or caching only after you've validated the feature with real users.

As a PM, resist the temptation to over-engineer v1. The fastest path to learning is getting real users interacting with Claude and measuring what works.

sections.title: Draft an AI Feature PRD
sections.content: Now put your AI product strategy skills to work by drafting a real PRD section for a Claude-powered feature.

exercise.prompt:
Pick a product you use regularly (or one you're building). Draft a PRD section for one AI-powered feature that uses Claude. Your PRD section should include:

1. **Problem Statement** — What user pain point does this feature address? Why is Claude the right solution?
2. **Feature Description** — What does the feature do? How does the user interact with it?
3. **Success Metrics** — Define 3 metrics across different layers (output quality, user experience, and business impact).
4. **Scope and Tradeoffs** — Which Claude model would you use and why? What are the key tradeoffs you're making in v1?
5. **Stakeholder Summary** — Write a 2-sentence description of this feature for your VP of Product who doesn't have a technical background.

Be specific and practical — avoid generic statements like "it will improve efficiency."
hints: Choose a feature where Claude's strengths (language understanding, analysis, generation) match a real user need
For success metrics, think about acceptance rate, edit distance, and task completion — not just accuracy
When choosing a model, consider the tradeoff between quality, latency, and cost for your specific use case
Your stakeholder summary should focus on the user outcome, not the technology
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A specific, well-defined problem statement that connects a real user pain point to Claude's capabilities — not a generic 'AI would be cool here' pitch. (2) A clear feature description with enough detail to understand the user interaction model. (3) Three layered success metrics — at least one measuring output quality (e.g., accuracy, hallucination rate), one measuring user experience (e.g., acceptance rate, time saved), and one measuring business impact (e.g., retention, cost per outcome). (4) A justified model selection (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) with reasoning about the cost-latency-quality tradeoff and at least one explicit v1 scope tradeoff. (5) A stakeholder summary that communicates value without jargon. Deduct points for vague metrics like 'improved efficiency' without specifics, or for choosing a model without justification.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
AI product strategy for PMs comes down to four principles:

• **Start with the problem, not the technology.** Every AI feature on your roadmap should trace back to a specific user pain point where Claude's capabilities are a genuine fit.

• **Measure what matters.** Output quality metrics tell you if Claude is working. User experience metrics tell you if it's useful. Business impact metrics tell you if it's worth it. You need all three layers.

• **Communicate with precision.** Set realistic expectations with every stakeholder group. Frame AI as a capability that improves over time, not a magic switch you flip on.

• **Scope for learning, not perfection.** Ship a prompt-based v1 fast, measure real user behavior, and iterate. The biggest risk in AI product development isn't shipping something imperfect — it's spending six months building infrastructure for a feature users don't want.
